## Turn 4 — Proposition 3.4 should be deleted, not repaired

Turn 3’s construction survives a scorched-earth audit. More importantly, it proves a structural statement stronger than the orientation-corrected Proposition 3.4, so the manuscript need not normalize an arbitrary relator at all.

The current Proposition 3.4 is false without the orientation hypothesis, while the manuscript treats it as its unique unresolved insertion point.  

### 1. A rigorous form of the Turn 3 construction

Put
\[
D_0=\langle A,S,Y\mid A^2S^4[S,Y]=1\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}.
\]
The relation is
\[
Y^{-1}SY=T,\qquad T=S^{-3}A^{-2}.
\]
Set
\[
P=S^3,\qquad C=(PT)^{-1}=A^2.
\]

The key input is the following uniform statement.

**Peripheral-power lemma.**  
For every \(u\in\mathbf Z_2^\times\), there is
\[
\varphi_u\in\operatorname{Aut}
 \langle P,T\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}
\]
such that
\[
\varphi_u(P)\sim P^u,\qquad
\varphi_u(T)\sim T^u,\qquad
\varphi_u(C)\sim C^u,
\qquad C=(PT)^{-1}.
\]

Indeed, identify the free pro-\(2\) group on \(P,T\) with
\[
\pi_1^{(2)}
 \bigl((\mathbf P^1-\{0,1,\infty\})_{\overline{\mathbf Q}}\bigr),
\]
with \(P,T,C\) the three peripheral inertia generators. The cyclotomic character
\[
\chi_2:G_{\mathbf Q}\twoheadrightarrow\mathbf Z_2^\times
\]
is surjective because
\[
\operatorname{Gal}\bigl(\mathbf Q(\mu_{2^\infty})/\mathbf Q\bigr)
 \cong\mathbf Z_2^\times.
\]
Choose \(g\) with \(\chi_2(g)=u\). At a rational cusp, pro-\(2\) inertia is \(\mathbf Z_2(1)\), so conjugation by \(g\) acts on it by multiplication by \(u\). Thus the induced outer automorphism powers all three peripheral conjugacy classes by \(u\); choose any representative.

Write
\[
\varphi_u(P)=c_P^{-1}P^uc_P,\quad
\varphi_u(T)=c_T^{-1}T^uc_T,\quad
\varphi_u(C)=c_C^{-1}C^uc_C.
\]

Now consider
\[
E_\square
 =\langle P,T,A\mid PTA^2=1\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}
 \cong\langle P,A\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}.
\]
Define
\[
P\longmapsto\varphi_u(P),\qquad
T\longmapsto\varphi_u(T),\qquad
A\longmapsto c_C^{-1}A^uc_C.
\]
The relation is preserved because
\[
(c_C^{-1}A^uc_C)^2
 =c_C^{-1}C^uc_C
 =\varphi_u(C)
 =(\varphi_u(P)\varphi_u(T))^{-1}.
\]
Modulo the Frattini subgroup, the free basis \(P,A\) is sent to
\[
\bar P\longmapsto u\bar P=\bar P,\qquad
\bar A\longmapsto u\bar A=\bar A.
\]
Consequently this endomorphism is an automorphism; denote it by \(\theta_u\).

No assertion that the abstract pair-of-pants group embeds as a particular closed subgroup is needed here: all conjugators are simply evaluated as words in the presentation of \(E_\square\).

Since \(P=S^3\), and odd powering is bijective on procyclic pro-\(2\) groups,
\[
\theta_u(S)=c_P^{-1}S^uc_P,\qquad
\theta_u(T)=c_T^{-1}T^uc_T.
\]
It therefore extends to \(D_0\) by
\[
\Psi_u|_{\langle S,A\rangle}=\theta_u,
\qquad
\Psi_u(Y)=c_P^{-1}Yc_T.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\Psi_u(Y)^{-1}\Psi_u(S)\Psi_u(Y)
 =c_T^{-1}Y^{-1}S^uYc_T
 =c_T^{-1}T^uc_T
 =\Psi_u(T).
\]
Its Frattini action is triangular with diagonal entries \(1,1,1\), so \(\Psi_u\) is an automorphism.

This supplies every unit \(u\) at once, rather than treating \(u=5\) separately. It is exactly the structural construction isolated in Turn 3. 

### 2. The complete automorphism statement

Let
\[
B=D_0^{\mathrm{ab}}
 =\langle\bar A,\bar S,\bar Y
       \mid2\bar A+4\bar S=0\rangle_{\mathbf Z_2},
\qquad
t=\bar A+2\bar S.
\]
Then
\[
B\cong C_2t\oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar S\oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar Y.
\]

The canonical orientation is
\[
\chi_0(t)=-1,\qquad
\chi_0(\bar S)=1,\qquad
\chi_0(\bar Y)=\eta:=(-3)^{-1}.
\]
Since \(v_2(\eta-1)=2\), \(\eta\) topologically generates \(1+4\mathbf Z_2\). Hence
\[
\ker\chi_0=\mathbf Z_2\bar S.
\]
It follows that every orientation-preserving automorphism of \(B\) has the unique form
\[
\alpha_{u,b}:
\quad
t\mapsto t,\qquad
\bar S\mapsto u\bar S,\qquad
\bar Y\mapsto\bar Y+b\bar S,
\]
where \(u\in\mathbf Z_2^\times\) and \(b\in\mathbf Z_2\).

The automorphism \(\Psi_u\) induces some \(\alpha_{u,b(u)}\). The shear
\[
\Theta_b(A)=A^{S^b},\qquad
\Theta_b(S)=S,\qquad
\Theta_b(Y)=YS^b
\]
satisfies
\[
\Theta_b\bigl(A^2S^4[S,Y]\bigr)
 =\bigl(A^2S^4[S,Y]\bigr)^{S^b}
\]
and induces \(\alpha_{1,b}\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Aut}(D_0)\longrightarrow
\operatorname{Aut}(B,\chi_0)
\]
is surjective.

This is the actual theorem needed by the manuscript.

### 3. Direct proof of the marked local normalization

The relator-normalization formulation can now be bypassed entirely.

Local reciprocity gives the marked abelianization
\[
D^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong B
\]
for \(D=G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)\), with
\[
\bar a=\operatorname{rec}(-4),\qquad
\bar s=\operatorname{rec}(1/2),\qquad
\bar y=\operatorname{rec}(-3).
\]
In addition to the existing unramified row, Lemma 3.5 should record
\[
\chi_D(\bar a,\bar s,\bar y)
 =\bigl(-1,1,(-3)^{-1}\bigr).
\]
Here the uniformizer component acts trivially on the totally ramified \(2\)-power cyclotomic extension, while for \(v\in\mathbf Z_2^\times\),
\[
\chi_D(\operatorname{rec}(v))=v^{-1}.
\]

Labute’s abstract classification—already invoked later in the manuscript—gives an isomorphism
\[
f:D\xrightarrow{\sim}D_0.
\]
Under the two identifications of the abelianization with \(B\), its induced map
\[
f_{\mathrm{ab}}\in\operatorname{Aut}(B)
\]
preserves \(\chi_0\), because the canonical Demushkin orientation is functorial:
\[
\chi_0\circ f_{\mathrm{ab}}=\chi_D=\chi_0.
\]
By the surjectivity above, choose
\[
\gamma\in\operatorname{Aut}(D_0),
\qquad
\gamma_{\mathrm{ab}}=f_{\mathrm{ab}}^{-1}.
\]
Then
\[
\beta=\gamma f:D\xrightarrow{\sim}D_0
\]
acts identically on \(B\).

Finally set
\[
a=\beta^{-1}(A),\qquad
s=\beta^{-1}(S),\qquad
y=\beta^{-1}(Y).
\]
Immediately,
\[
a^2s^4[s,y]=1
\]
and, because \(\beta_{\mathrm{ab}}=1\),
\[
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(a,s,y)=(-2,1,0).
\]
This proves Proposition 1.1 directly.

There is no need to choose a free presentation, normalize the abelianized exponent of a relator, compare initial forms, or lift an isomorphism back to a free-group automorphism. The Hilbert-symbol computation may remain as a consistency check and for the displayed cup table, but it is no longer load-bearing for the marked normalization.

### Manuscript repair

The clean revision is therefore:

1. Replace Proposition 3.4 by the surjectivity theorem
   \[
   \operatorname{Aut}(D_0)\twoheadrightarrow
   \operatorname{Aut}(D_0^{\mathrm{ab}},\chi_0).
   \]
2. Insert the peripheral-power and square-root lemmas above.
3. Add the orientation row to Lemma 3.5.
4. Move the abstract Labute classification statement from Proposition 3.7 to immediately before the proof of Proposition 1.1.
5. Replace the current “Derivation … from Lemma 3.5 and Proposition 3.4” by the three-line adjusted-isomorphism argument.
6. Delete the conditional-status language in §3.1 and §11.

The displayed four-generator candidate and every subsequent finite-quotient argument remain unchanged. The manuscript itself says the full marked pro-\(2\) boundary is the only downstream use of the missing input, so this direct marked isomorphism supplies exactly what is required. 

ESTABLISHED — The false relator-normalization Proposition 3.4 can be removed entirely. A uniform rooted pair-of-pants construction proves \(\operatorname{Aut}(D_0)\twoheadrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(B,\chi_0)\), and Labute classification plus local reciprocity then proves the fully marked local presentation directly.

GAP — Only manuscript integration remains: formalize the standard cusp-inertia action, add the orientation row, reorder the abstract classification, and remove the conditional-status text.

NEXT — Patch §3.1 and §11 using the direct marked-isomorphism proof; do not retain the obsolete initial-form hypotheses as part of the normalization argument.

The Turn 4 strategy survives, with one essential correction: the shear must be
[
\Theta_b(A)=A^{S^b},\qquad \Theta_b(S)=S,\qquad \Theta_b(Y)=YS^b,
]
not (AS^b) if the history’s superscript is read literally. Indeed,
[
[S,YS^b]=[S,Y]^{S^b}
]
and therefore
[
\Theta_b!\left(A^2S^4[S,Y]\right)
=(A^2)^{S^b}S^4[S,Y]^{S^b}
=\left(A^2S^4[S,Y]\right)^{S^b}.
]
Thus it descends to the standard Demushkin group and induces precisely the required abelianized shear (\bar Y\mapsto\bar Y+b\bar S).

I integrated the direct argument into the manuscript, replacing the false un-oriented Proposition 3.4 that the original text treated as its sole insertion point.  The downstream proof only requires the resulting fully marked maximal pro-(2) identification, so the finite-quotient argument and the displayed candidate relation are unchanged. 

### Structural replacement

For
[
D_0=\langle A,S,Y\mid A^2S^4[S,Y]=1\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2},
]
write
[
B=D_0^{\mathrm{ab}},\qquad
t=\bar A+2\bar S,
\qquad
\eta=(-3)^{-1}.
]
The standard orientation satisfies
[
\chi_0(t)=-1,\qquad \chi_0(\bar S)=1,\qquad
\chi_0(\bar Y)=\eta.
]
Since (v_2(\eta-1)=2), (\eta) generates (1+4\mathbf Z_2), and hence
[
\ker\chi_0=\mathbf Z_2\bar S.
]
Consequently every element of (\operatorname{Aut}(B,\chi_0)) is uniquely
[
t\mapsto t,\qquad
\bar S\mapsto u\bar S,\qquad
\bar Y\mapsto\bar Y+b\bar S
]
with (u\in\mathbf Z_2^\times) and (b\in\mathbf Z_2).

The unit direction is lifted uniformly. Rewriting the relation as
[
Y^{-1}SY=T,\qquad T=S^{-3}A^{-2},
]
put
[
P=S^3,\qquad C=(PT)^{-1}=A^2.
]
The cyclotomic outer action on the geometric pro-(2) fundamental group of
(\mathbf P^1-{0,1,\infty}) gives, for every (u\in\mathbf Z_2^\times), an automorphism of the free pro-(2) group on (P,T) powering all three peripheral conjugacy classes (P,T,C) by (u).

It lifts through
[
E_\square=\langle P,T,A\mid PTA^2=1\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}
\cong\langle P,A\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}
]
by sending a conjugate expression for (C^u) to the square of the corresponding conjugate of (A^u). The Frattini action fixes the free basis, so the lift is an automorphism. Extending across (Y^{-1}SY=T) then produces (\Psi_u\in\operatorname{Aut}(D_0)). Its abelianized action is some (\alpha_{u,b(u)}); the corrected shears (\Theta_b) remove the unwanted (b(u)). Hence
[
\operatorname{Aut}(D_0)\twoheadrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(B,\chi_0).
]

### Direct marked local normalization

The local reciprocity classes already used by the manuscript satisfy
[
\bar a=\operatorname{rec}(-4),\qquad
\bar s=\operatorname{rec}(1/2),\qquad
\bar y=\operatorname{rec}(-3),
]
and give the stated marked abelianization and Hilbert pairing.  I added the missing orientation row
[
\chi_D(\bar a,\bar s,\bar y)
=\bigl(-1,1,(-3)^{-1}\bigr).
]
Labute classification supplies an abstract isomorphism (f:D\to D_0). Functoriality of the canonical orientation gives
[
\chi_0\circ f^{\mathrm{ab}}=\chi_D=\chi_0,
]
so (f^{\mathrm{ab}}\in\operatorname{Aut}(B,\chi_0)). Choose
[
\gamma\in\operatorname{Aut}(D_0),
\qquad
\gamma^{\mathrm{ab}}=(f^{\mathrm{ab}})^{-1}.
]
Then (\beta=\gamma f) acts identically on (B). The elements
[
a=\beta^{-1}(A),\qquad s=\beta^{-1}(S),\qquad y=\beta^{-1}(Y)
]
therefore satisfy
[
a^2s^4[s,y]=1,\qquad
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(a,s,y)=(-2,1,0).
]
This proves Proposition 1.1 without arbitrary-relator normalization, initial-form correction, or lifting an isomorphism back to a free-group automorphism, exactly as anticipated in Turn 4.

# Edit and addition plan

Use **`q2_presentation_turn5_checked.tex` as the sole base manuscript**. The reviewers’ complete `.tex` files should be treated as donor drafts, not replacements: each repairs a different subset of the proof, and none contains the full union of the valid corrections.

The marked Demuškin normalization in Section 3 should remain essentially frozen. The remaining work is concentrated in Sections 5, 6, 8, and 9. The current manuscript still relies on an unsupported cellular interpretation in Proposition 5.7, an invalid dismissal of the \(H^1(H,V^\vee)\) ambiguity in Lemma 6.3, an asserted rather than constructed half-orbit/Evens comparison, and a compressed “half the torsor” claim in Lemma 8.6.  

## 1. Preserve without substantive alteration

The following should not be redesigned:

- the presentation and all auxiliary words;
- the machine-readable serialization;
- the repaired Section 3 marked Demuškin proof;
- the common boundary of Section 4;
- the Fox Jacobian and mixed-Hessian calculations through Lemma 5.6;
- the Wall-doubling calculation;
- the finite-target induction architecture and all numerical recursion formulas.

The candidate wild relation and serialization block must remain byte-for-byte unchanged. The later research turns also concluded that the induction architecture is structurally sound once determinant canonicality, the radical edge, and the \(R\)-module wording are repaired. 

---

# 2. Replace the cellular Stokes argument in Section 5.2

## 2.1 Remove the unsupported identification

Delete the claim that the central coordinate of finite Heisenberg word evaluation is “exactly the Alexander–Whitney cellular cup product” on the presentation complex.

Replace the introductory sentence after the Heisenberg multiplication formulas by:

> These are the finite Fox recursion formulas. The Stokes identities below are proved directly by conjugation in the finite Heisenberg semidirect product; no cellular diagonal approximation is used.

This directly addresses Reviewer 3’s criticism without changing the resulting chain map.

## 2.2 Insert a finite-word Stokes lemma

Immediately after strict coefficient naturality, insert a new lemma.

### New lemma: finite-word Stokes formula

Let
\[
F_n=\langle g_1,\dots,g_n\rangle,\qquad \rho(g_i)=c_i\in C,
\]
and let \(r\in F_n\) be an ordinary finite word with \(\rho(r)=1\). Let
\(\epsilon_i(r)\in\mathbf F_2\) be the total exponent of \(g_i\) modulo \(2\).

For \(x=(x_i)\in A^n\) and \(y=(y_i)\in(A^\vee)^n\), write
\[
r(x,y)=
\bigl(L_r^A(x),L_r^{A^\vee}(y),\beta_r(x,y);1\bigr)
\]
after evaluation in \(\mathcal H(A)\rtimes C\). Then
\[
\beta_r(d^0a,y)
=
\langle a,L_r^{A^\vee}(y)\rangle
+
\sum_i\epsilon_i(r)y_i(c_i a),
\]
and
\[
\beta_r(x,d^0\lambda)
=
\langle L_r^A(x),\lambda\rangle
+
\sum_i\epsilon_i(r)\lambda(x_i).
\]

The proof must be an explicit conjugation calculation. With
\[
p_a=(a,0,0;1),\qquad p_\lambda=(0,\lambda,0;1),
\]
record
\[
p_a^{-1}(0,y_i,0;c_i)p_a
=
((c_i-1)a,y_i,0;c_i)\,
\mathbf z\bigl(y_i(c_i a)\bigr),
\]
and
\[
p_\lambda^{-1}(x_i,0,0;c_i)p_\lambda
=
(x_i,(c_i-1)\lambda,0;c_i)\,
\mathbf z\bigl(\lambda(x_i)\bigr).
\]
Because \(\mathbf z(t)\) is central of exponent \(2\), its contribution to a word is exactly weighted by \(\epsilon_i(r)\).

Add the immediate traced consequence: if words \(r_j\) and trace coefficients \(t_j\in\mathbf F_2\) satisfy
\[
\sum_jt_j\epsilon_i(r_j)=0
\quad\text{for every }i,
\]
then the correction terms cancel and the two Stokes identities hold for the traced mixed coordinate.

## 2.3 Replace Proposition 5.7’s proof

Rename it:

> **Traced finite-word Stokes identity**

Choose one odd integer representative of \(\omega_2\) valid in the finite groups used in the calculation. Compute the exponent vectors in the ordered generators
\((\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1)\):
\[
\epsilon(r_{\mathrm t})=(0,1,0,0),
\qquad
\epsilon(r_{\mathrm w})=(0,1,0,0).
\]

The proof should spell out:

- \(r_{\mathrm t}=\tau^\sigma\tau^{-2}\) has odd total \(\tau\)-exponent;
- \(c_0\) and \(h_c\) are commutators;
- in
  \[
  h_0=x_0^{g_0}x_0\,d_gd_0d_0^2h_c,
  \]
  the two \(x_0\)-terms cancel modulo \(2\), the \(d_g,d_0\) terms occur twice, and \(d_0^2\) has even exponent vector;
- \(u_1^{-1}\) contributes \(x_1+\tau\), while \(x_1^\sigma\) contributes \(x_1\).

Thus the two exponent vectors sum to zero, and the new finite-word lemma gives the required identities.

Retain the existing \(\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbf F_2)\) regression test, but replace “two-cell” by “relator.”

## 2.4 Update Proposition 5.9 and Appendix A

The Fox–Heisenberg chain-map statement remains unchanged. Its proof should cite the new finite-word lemma, not cellular Leibniz or Alexander–Whitney machinery.

The final paragraph of Appendix A should say that the finite-word correction terms cancel only after summing the two relators. Remove every remaining claim about a cellular diagonal approximation.

---

# 3. Rebuild the determinant base class explicitly in Section 6

The current text says that the half-orbit class is “the transfer” of a two-point class, but does not give the cocycle or prove the Shapiro–Evens identification. 

## 3.1 Insert an explicit involution-orbit cocycle

Immediately after the equivariant extraspecial connecting-cocycle lemma, insert:

### New lemma: explicit involution-orbit cocycle

Let \(g\in H\) be an involution and let
\[
M=\mathbf F_2[H],
\qquad
(cx)_h=x_{c^{-1}h}.
\]
Choose representatives \(\mathcal R\) for the right cosets
\(u\langle g\rangle\). For \(c\in H\), write
\[
c^{-1}u=\pi_c(u)g^{\epsilon_c(u)},
\qquad
\pi_c(u)\in\mathcal R,\quad
\epsilon_c(u)\in\mathbf F_2.
\]
Define
\[
q_g(x)=\sum_{u\in\mathcal R}x_u x_{ug},
\]
\[
f_g(x,y)=\sum_{u\in\mathcal R}x_u y_{ug},
\]
and
\[
m_c^g(x)=
\sum_{u\in\mathcal R}
\epsilon_c(u)x_{\pi_c(u)}x_{\pi_c(u)g}.
\]

Prove
\[
m_c^g(x+y)+m_c^g(x)+m_c^g(y)
=
f_g(cx,cy)+f_g(x,y),
\]
and
\[
m_{cd}^g(x)=m_c^g(dx)+m_d^g(x),
\qquad m_1^g=0.
\]

The proof should explicitly derive
\[
\pi_{cd}=\pi_d\circ\pi_c,
\qquad
\epsilon_{cd}(u)
=
\epsilon_c(u)+\epsilon_d(\pi_c(u)),
\]
and distinguish whether \(c\) preserves or reverses the orientation of each two-point block.

Conclude that
\[
\kappa_g^{\mathcal R}((x,c),(y,d))
=
f_g(x,cy)+m_c^g(y)
\]
is a normalized \(2\)-cocycle on \(M\rtimes H\), vanishes on the zero section, and restricts to the square map \(q_g\).

## 3.2 Replace the base determinant construction

Rewrite the existing base-class lemma so that it constructs actual cocycles, not merely orbit polynomials.

For
\[

# 1. Expand the Fox–Heisenberg dévissage into an exact cone argument

Insert a lemma immediately before candidate deformation duality.

## Exact cone dévissage

For an elementary \(C\)-module \(A\), put
\[
K(A)=\operatorname{Cone}\!\left(
D_A:C^\bullet_{\rho,A}\longrightarrow
\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf F_2}
(C^\bullet_{\rho,A^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
\right).
\]

For every short exact sequence
\[
0\longrightarrow A'\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow A''\longrightarrow0
\]
there is a degreewise exact sequence
\[
0\longrightarrow K(A')
\longrightarrow K(A)
\longrightarrow K(A'')
\longrightarrow0.
\]

### Proof to add

The source complexes form a degreewise exact sequence
\[
0\to C^\bullet_{\rho,A'}
 \to C^\bullet_{\rho,A}
 \to C^\bullet_{\rho,A''}\to0.
\]
Dualizing the coefficient sequence gives
\[
0\to A''^\vee\to A^\vee\to A'^\vee\to0.
\]
Applying the word-complex functor and then \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbf F_2)\) reverses it again, giving
\[
0\to
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A'^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
\to
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
\to
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A''^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
\to0.
\]
Strict coefficient naturality of finite Heisenberg evaluation gives the commutative diagram
\[
\begin{CD}
0@>>>C^\bullet_{\rho,A'}@>>>C^\bullet_{\rho,A}
   @>>>C^\bullet_{\rho,A''}@>>>0\\
@.@V D_{A'}VV@V D_A VV@V D_{A''}VV@.\\
0@>>>
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A'^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
@>>>
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
@>>>
\operatorname{Hom}(C^\bullet_{\rho,A''^\vee},\mathbf F_2)[-2]
@>>>0 .
\end{CD}
\]
Taking mapping cones degree by degree proves the asserted exact sequence. Its long exact cohomology sequence shows that if two of \(K(A'),K(A),K(A'')\) are acyclic, so is the third. Therefore a composition series reduces the quasi-isomorphism assertion to simple modules, including nonsplit and nonsemisimple \(A\).

## Also spell out the simple-module normal forms

For a nontrivial simple tame module there are exactly two cases.

When \(T=1\), \(P=1\), and \(1+S^{-1}\) is invertible. Directly from the displayed Jacobian,
\[
Z^1_{\rho}(V)
 =
 \{(a,0,c,0):a,c\in V\},
\]
while
\[
B^1_{\rho}(V)
 =
 \{((S-1)v,0,0,0):v\in V\}.
\]
Since \(S-1\) is invertible, each cohomology class has a unique representative
\[
(0,0,c,0).
\]

When \(V^T=0\), \(P=0\), and \(T-1=1+T\) is invertible. The wild row first forces \(d=0\). Given a cocycle \((a,b,c,0)\), subtract the coboundary of
\[
v=(T-1)^{-1}b.
\]
This kills \(b\); the tame row then forces \(a=0\). Again every class has a unique representative
\[
(0,0,c,0).
\]

On these representatives the degree-one pairing is
\[
(c,\lambda)\longmapsto
\begin{cases}
\lambda(c),&T=1,\\[2mm]
\lambda\bigl((1+U+U^{-1})c\bigr),&V^T=0.
\end{cases}
\]
In the second case \(U\) has \(2\)-power order, so its minimal polynomial divides a power of \(X+1\). Since
\[
X^2+X+1
\]
is coprime to \(X+1\), the operator
\[
1+U+U^{-1}=U^{-1}(U^2+U+1)
\]
is invertible. This proves the quasi-isomorphism on every simple constituent and makes the cone dévissage completely formal.

That is the expansion which answers the objection to the possibly nonsemisimple coefficient case.

# 2. Replace the ramified Arf paragraph by a Hermitian-model lemma

The current conclusion is plausible, but the reviewer is right that the two assertions
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q)\equiv s\pmod2,
\qquad
\dim V^U=rs
\]
should be proved rather than announced. 

Insert the following standalone result before the candidate zero-count proposition.

## Ramified Hermitian model and fixed-space calculation

Let \(I=\langle T\rangle\) be tame inertia. Let \(V\) be a simple, ramified, self-dual \(\mathbf F_2[H]\)-module, and write
\[
V|_I\simeq W^{\oplus s},
\qquad
f=\dim_{\mathbf F_2}W=2^a r,
\quad r\ \text{odd},\ a\ge1.
\]
Let \(q\) be an \(H\)-invariant nonsingular quadratic form, with polar form \(B\).

Then:

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q)\equiv s\pmod2,
\]
and for \(U=S^{\omega _2}\),
\[
\dim_{\mathbf F_2}V^U=rs,
\qquad
\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
=rs(2^a-1)\equiv s\pmod2.
\]

### Hermitian part of the proof

Let
\[
D=\operatorname{End}_I(W)\simeq\mathbf F_{2^f}.
\]
Choosing an inertia character \(\zeta\) realizes \(W\) as the additive group of \(D\), with \(T\) acting by multiplication by \(\zeta\).

Self-duality means that \(\zeta^{-1}\) belongs to the Frobenius orbit of \(\zeta\). Hence \(f\) is even and
\[
\zeta^{-1}=\zeta^{2^{f/2}}.
\]
Put
\[
D_0=\mathbf F_{2^{f/2}},
\qquad
x^*=x^{2^{f/2}}.
\]
After choosing an \(I\)-module identification \(V\simeq D^s\), invariance and nondegeneracy of \(B\) give a unique nonsingular Hermitian matrix
\[
H\in\operatorname{GL}_s(D),
\qquad H^{*t}=H,
\]
such that
\[
B(x,y)=
\operatorname{Tr}_{D/\mathbf F_2}(x^{*t}Hy).
\]
Define
\[
q_H(x)=
\operatorname{Tr}_{D_0/\mathbf F_2}(x^{*t}Hx).
\]
Since \(x^{*t}Hx\in D_0\), this is well-defined. Its polarization is
\[
\begin{aligned}
q_H(x+y)+q_H(x)+q_H(y)
&=
\operatorname{Tr}_{D_0/\mathbf F_2}
   (x^{*t}Hy+y^{*t}Hx)\\
&=
\operatorname{Tr}_{D/\mathbf F_2}(x^{*t}Hy)
=B(x,y).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(q-q_H\) is linear. It is \(I\)-invariant, but
\[
(V^\vee)^I=0
\]
because \(V\) is ramified. Hence \(q=q_H\).

Hermitian diagonalization gives
\[
H\simeq\operatorname{diag}(a_1,\dots ,a_s),
\qquad a_i\in D_0^\times.
\]
Thus \(q\) is an orthogonal sum of the \(s\) forms
\[
q_{a_i}(x)=
\operatorname{Tr}_{D_0/\mathbf F_2}(a_i xx^*).
\]
For one such line, write \(m=f/2\). The norm map
\[
D^\times\longrightarrow D_0^\times
\]
has fibres of size \(2^m+1\), and the trace-zero subspace of \(D_0\) has
\(2^{m-1}\) elements. Consequently
\[
\#q_{a_i}^{-1}(0)
 =
1+(2^m+1)(2^{m-1}-1)
 =
2^{2m-1}-2^{m-1}.
\]
Each Hermitian line therefore has Arf invariant \(1\), so additivity of the Arf invariant gives
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(q)\equiv s\pmod2.
\]

### \(U\)-fixed-space part of the proof

Over \(\overline{\mathbf F}_2\), the \(f\) inertia weights form one Frobenius cycle, each weight space having dimension \(s\).

Let \(e_\omega\) be the residue of \(\omega _2\) modulo \(f=2^ar\). Then
\[
e_\omega\equiv1\pmod{2^a},
\qquad
e_\omega\equiv0\pmod r.
\]
Thus translation by \(e_\omega\) on \(\mathbf Z/f\mathbf Z\) has
\[
\gcd(f,e_\omega)=r
\]
cycles, each of length \(2^a\). Hence \(U\) permutes the inertia-weight spaces in \(r\) cycles of length \(2^a\).

On one cycle write the successive linear maps as
\[
E_0\xrightarrow{A_0}E_1
\xrightarrow{A_1}\cdots
\xrightarrow{A_{2^a-1}}E_0,
\qquad \dim E_i=s.
\]
The product
\[
A_{2^a-1}\cdots A_0
\]
is the action of \(U^{2^a}\) on \(E_0\). The element \(U^{2^a}\) commutes with inertia and with \(S\), hence is central in the faithful image. It is a \(2\)-element. A central \(2\)-element acting on a simple characteristic-\(2\) module acts trivially: it has nonzero fixed vectors, and its fixed space is an \(H\)-submodule. Therefore
\[
A_{2^a-1}\cdots A_0=1.
\]
A \(U\)-fixed vector on the cycle is uniquely determined by its component in \(E_0\), so the fixed space contributed by that cycle has dimension \(s\). Hence
\[
\dim V^U=rs.
\]
It follows that
\[
\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
=fs-rs
=rs(2^a-1)
\equiv s\pmod2.
\]

Combining this with the Wall identity gives
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_A^0)
=
\operatorname{Arf}(q)+\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
=0.
\]

This is the complete representation-theoretic fill-in the reviewers are requesting.

# 3. Consolidate the Evens/Shapiro chain into one normalization theorem

The manuscript now has many of the formulas, but the reviewer is still being asked to follow a chain spread over several lemmas. The best fix is a single theorem showing that three constructions are literally the same class:

1. the explicit two-point cocycle;
2. the standard degree-two Evens norm;
3. the relative Stiefel–Whitney class used in the dyadic calculation.

Evens’s multiplicative transfer is the primary external construction, while Kahn and Kozlowski give the relevant Stiefel–Whitney comparison. 

## Universal two-point normalization theorem

Let
\[
J=\langle s\rangle\simeq C_2,
\qquad
E=\mathbf F_2e_0\oplus\mathbf F_2e_1,
\qquad
s(x_0,x_1)=(x_1,x_0).
\]
Define
\[
q_{\mathrm{hyp}}(x_0,x_1)=x_0x_1,
\qquad
f(x,y)=x_0y_1,
\]
and
\[
m_1=0,\qquad
m_s(y)=y_0y_1.
\]
Then
\[
\kappa_J((x,c),(y,d))
=f(x,cy)+m_c(y)
\]
is a normalized \(2\)-cocycle on \(E\rtimes J\). It restricts to \(q_{\mathrm{hyp}}\) on \(E\) and to zero on \(J\).

The associated fibre extension is \(D_8\): the two coordinate lifts are involutions, their commutator is the central involution, and their product has square equal to that involution.

Moreover,
\[
[\kappa_J]
=
N_E^{E\rtimes J,\mathrm{Ev}}(e_0^\vee).
\]

### Proof of the equality with the standard norm

First,
\[
H^1(J,E^\vee)
=
\frac{\ker(1+s)}{\operatorname{im}(1+s)}
=0.
\]
The low-degree Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre sequence therefore shows that a class on \(E\rtimes J\) is uniquely determined by:

\[
\text{its restriction to }E,
\qquad
\text{its restriction to the zero section }J.
\]

For the standard Evens norm of the first coordinate class \(e_0^\vee\),

\[
\operatorname{res}_E
N_E^{E\rtimes J,\mathrm{Ev}}(e_0^\vee)
=
e_0^\vee\, e_1^\vee,
\]
while the relative normalization gives zero on \(J\). These are exactly the two restrictions of \(\kappa_J\), so uniqueness gives the equality.

Now let \(N\triangleleft G\) have index \(2\), let
\(\alpha\in Z^1(N,\mathbf F_2)\), and let
\[
b=\operatorname{Sh}_N^G(\alpha)\in Z^1(G,E).
\]
The graph
\[
\Phi_\alpha:G\longrightarrow E\rtimes J,
\qquad
\gamma\longmapsto(b(\gamma),\bar\gamma)
\]
fits into a pullback square whose inverse image of \(E\) is \(N\). Naturality of the Evens norm gives
\[
\Phi_\alpha^*[\kappa_J]
=
N_N^{G,\mathrm{Ev}}([\alpha]).
\]
Direct substitution gives the normalized representative
\[
\nu_\alpha(\gamma,\eta)
=
b(\gamma)_0\,
b(\eta)_{\bar\gamma^{-1}1}
+
\varepsilon(\bar\gamma)\,
b(\eta)_0b(\eta)_1,
\]
with \(\varepsilon(1)=0\), \(\varepsilon(s)=1\).

This argument removes any possible ambiguity between “the class called the Evens norm” and the actual graph cocycle in the manuscript.

## Then derive the half-orbit formula functorially

For an involution \(g\in H\), put \(J=\langle g\rangle\). Let
\[
\pi_{r,g}:W\longrightarrow E
\]
select the two coordinates indexed by \(1\) and \(g\), and define
\[
[\kappa_{r,g}]
=
\operatorname{cor}_{W\rtimes J}^{W\rtimes H}
(\pi_{r,g}\rtimes1)^*[\kappa_J].
\]
Mackey gives
\[
\operatorname{res}_{W}[\kappa_{r,g}]
=
\sum_{u\in H/J}X_{r,u}X_{r,ug},
\]
and restriction to the zero section is zero.

For the Galois graph map \(\Phi_\alpha:G_F\to W\rtimes H\), the inverse image of \(W\rtimes J\) is \(G_{K_0}\), where
\[
K_0=K^J.
\]
Naturality of corestriction in the pullback square gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha^*[\kappa_{r,g}]
&=
\operatorname{cor}_{K_0/F}
\Phi_{\alpha,0}^*(\pi_{r,g}^*[\kappa_J])\\
&=
\operatorname{cor}_{K_0/F}
N_{K/K_0}^{\mathrm{Ev}}(\alpha_r).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the half-orbit formula as a chain of standard functorial equalities. The existing explicit normalized-corestriction calculation should then be retained in an appendix as a direct verification of this conceptual proof.

# 4. Turn the deep-unit proof into a calculation ledger

The existing calculation is essentially right, but a reviewer should not have to redo any simplification.

Start with
\[
a=1+2b,\qquad b\in\mathfrak p_L.
\]
Write
\[
L=k(\sqrt d),\qquad
a=u+v\sqrt d,\qquad
n=N_{L/k}(a).
\]
Then
\[
u=1+\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}(b)\in\mathcal O_k^\times.
\]

The trace forms are
\[
\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}\langle a\rangle
\simeq
\left\langle2u,\frac{2dn}{u}\right\rangle,
\qquad
\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}\langle1\rangle
\simeq\langle2,2d\rangle.
\]
The degree-two part of the relative Stiefel–Whitney identity is therefore
\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}([a])
=
(2u,2dn/u)+(2,2d)+(d,n).
\]
Now expand every term:
\[
\begin{aligned}
(2u,2dn/u)
&=(2u,2)+(2u,d)+(2u,n)+(2u,u),\\
(2u,2)+(2u,u)
&=(2u,2u)=(2u,-1),\\
(2,2d)&=(2,-1)+(2,d).
\end{aligned}
\]
After cancellation,
\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}([a])
=(u,-d)+(2du,n).
\]

Put
\[
t=\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}(b),
\qquad
m=N_{L/k}(b).
\]
Then
\[
u=1+t,\qquad
n=1+2t+4m,
\]
and
\[
\frac{n}{2u-1}
=
1+\frac{4m}{2u-1}.
\]
Because \(L/k\) is unramified and \(b\in\mathfrak p_L\),
\[
v_k(m)\ge2.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac{n}{2u-1}\in U_{2e+2}(k)\subset k^{\times2}.
\]
For the last inclusion, apply Hensel to
\[
F(X)=X^2-\frac{n}{2u-1}
\]
at \(X=1\):
\[
v_k(F(1))\ge2e+2>2v_k(F'(1))=2e.
\]
Thus \(n\) and \(2u-1\) have the same square class.

Since every unit is a norm from an unramified quadratic extension, and \(n\) is itself a norm,
\[
(u,d)=0,\qquad(d,n)=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}([a])
=(u,-1)+(2u,2u-1).
\]
The Steinberg relation gives
\[
(2u,2u-1)=(2u,-1).
\]
Finally,
\[
(2,-1)=0:
\]
if \(i\in k\), then \(-1\) is a square; otherwise
\[
N_{k(i)/k}(1+i)=2.
\]
Thus
\[
(2u,-1)=(u,-1),
\]
and the two terms cancel.

This should be displayed as a continuous derivation, not split between prose and references.

# 5. Correct and fully prove the determinant transgression lemma

This is the one place where I would change the statement, not merely lengthen the proof.

The blanket assertion
\[
d_2(q)=B_q^\flat{}_*\eta
\]
for every invariant \(q\) is too broad. For a split semidirect product, an invariant class need not automatically possess an equivariant lift. In this manuscript, however, the needed \(q\) already comes with the constructed zero-section-normalized class
\[
\kappa_q^0\in H^2(V\rtimes C,\mathbf F_2).
\]
State that hypothesis explicitly.

## Corrected transgression statement

Let \(q\) be a nonsingular \(C\)-invariant quadratic form on \(V\), and assume a zero-section-normalized equivariant class
\[
\kappa_q^0\in H^2(V\rtimes C,\mathbf F_2)
\]
restricting to \(q\) has been fixed.

Let
\[
1\to V\to B\to C\to1
\]
have extension class
\[
\eta\in H^2(C,V).
\]
Then the obstruction to transporting \(\kappa_q^0\) from the split extension \(V\rtimes C\) to \(B\) is
\[
B_q^\flat{}_*\eta\in H^2(C,V^\vee).
\]
Equivalently, in the LHS spectral sequence relative to the fixed equivariant lift,
\[
d_2(q)=B_q^\flat{}_*\eta.
\]

### Full extension-theoretic proof

Let
\[
1\to\mathbf F_2\to E_q\to V\to1
\]
be the central extension represented by \(\kappa_q^0|_V\).

Central automorphisms of \(E_q\) inducing the identity on \(V\) are canonically \(V^\vee\). Inner conjugation by a lift of \(v\in V\) corresponds to
\[
B_q^\flat(v)\in V^\vee,
\qquad
w\longmapsto B_q(v,w).
\]

Choose a normalized section \(s:C\to B\) and write
\[
s(c)s(d)=\eta(c,d)s(cd).
\]
The fixed equivariant class \(\kappa_q^0\) supplies coherent lifts
\[
\alpha_c\in\operatorname{Aut}(E_q)
\]
of the \(C\)-action, satisfying
\[
\alpha_c\alpha_d=\alpha_{cd}.
\]

To extend \(E_q\) over \(B\), lifts of the section elements would have to satisfy
\[
\widehat s(c)\widehat s(d)
=
\widehat{\eta(c,d)}\,\widehat s(cd)
\]
up to the central kernel. Compare conjugation on \(E_q\). The left side induces
\[
\alpha_c\alpha_d=\alpha_{cd}.
\]
The right side induces
\[
\operatorname{Int}(\widehat{\eta(c,d)})\alpha_{cd}.
\]
Their discrepancy is therefore the central automorphism
\[
w\longmapsto B_q(\eta(c,d),w),
\]
namely the \(V^\vee\)-valued cocycle
\[
B_q^\flat(\eta(c,d)).
\]

Changing the section \(s\) changes \(\eta\) by a coboundary and changes this cocycle by the corresponding \(V^\vee\)-coboundary. Changing the lifted action \(\alpha_c\) by central automorphisms \(\gamma(c)\in V^\vee\) changes the discrepancy by \(d\gamma\). Hence its cohomology class is exactly
\[
B_q^\flat{}_*[\eta].
\]
This is the LHS obstruction.

If \(q\) is the restriction of an actual class in \(H^2(B,\mathbf F_2)\), that obstruction vanishes. Since \(q\) is nonsingular,
\[
B_q^\flat:V\xrightarrow{\sim}V^\vee
\]
is a \(C\)-module isomorphism. Therefore
\[
[\eta]=0,
\]
so the extension \(B\to C\) splits.

This proof is more checkable than the present reference to one bicomplex component, and it avoids an overgeneral statement about arbitrary invariant quadratic classes. The original Hochschild–Serre construction is the standard spectral-sequence framework behind this obstruction calculation. 

# 6. Replace the “roadmap” proposition by a closed recursion theorem

The current Sections 8–9 contain the needed ingredients, but the reviewer sees Proposition 8.9 as an assertion that they fit together. The fix is to state the actual recursion in one place.

Let
\[
e_\Gamma(Y)
\]
denote the boundary-framed exact-image count.

Use the target notation
\[
R=\Phi(K),\qquad
M=K/R,\qquad
0\to T\to M\to V\to0,
\]
\[
C=Y/K,\qquad B=Y/R.
\]

Put
\[
D_R=(R^\vee)^C,
\qquad
z_R=|Z^1_{\Gamma,\rho}(R)|
   =2^{2\dim R+\dim D_R}.
\]
The value of \(z_R\) is independent of the lower exact-image map and common to the two sources.

For \(\lambda\in D_R\), let
\[
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B)
\]
be the number of exact-image maps to \(B\) for which the scalar pushout obstruction \(\lambda_*o_R\) vanishes.

## Exact recursion, first stage

Fourier inversion and the final \(R\)-lift torsor give
\[
\boxed{
e_\Gamma(Y)
=
\frac{z_R}{|D_R|}
\sum_{\lambda\in D_R}
\bigl(2m_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B)-e_\Gamma(B)\bigr).
}
\tag{R1}
\]
For \(\lambda=0\),
\[
m_{\Gamma,0}(B)=e_\Gamma(B).
\]

For \(\lambda\ne0\), let
\[
p_\lambda:B_\lambda\to B
\]
be the corresponding scalar central cover. Partitioning all compatible maps by their exact image gives
\[
\boxed{
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B)
=
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
-
\sum_{\substack{J<B\\J\twoheadrightarrow C}}
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}(J).
}
\tag{R2}
\]
For every proper \(J\),
\[
\boxed{
8m_{\Gamma,\lambda}(J)
=
\sum_{\substack{\widetilde J\le p_\lambda^{-1}(J)\\
p_\lambda(\widetilde J)=J}}
e_\Gamma(\widetilde J).
}
\tag{R3}
\]
This is a literal partition of the eight lifts under
\[
\operatorname{Hom}(\Gamma,\mathbf F_2).
\]

## Exact recursion, nonzero radical edge

If the radical edge is nonzero, the variation formula is a nonzero affine functional on every \(T\)-lift torsor. Consequently
\[
\boxed{
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
2^{2\dim M-1}e_\Gamma(C).
}
\tag{R4a}
\]

## Exact recursion, zero radical edge

Suppose the edge is zero. Put
\[
D=(T^\vee)^C,
\qquad r=\dim D,
\qquad d=\dim V,
\]
and
\[
\mu=
|B^1_{\Gamma,\rho}(V)|
\,|Z^1_{\Gamma,\rho}(T)|.
\]
This is again independent of \(\rho\) and common to both sources.

For each \(\chi\in D\), let
\[
\Delta_{\chi,\kappa}
=
\chi_*e+\delta_\kappa+
\Theta_q^0(a_{\chi,\kappa})
\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2).
\]
Then the constrained Gauss formula gives
\[
\boxed{
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
